name: Eir
description: Eir is a Norse goddess associated with healing and medicine. Her name, meaning 'help' or 'mercy,' reflects her primary role as a skilled healer. While mentions of Eir are relatively scarce in surviving Norse texts, she is considered one of the handmaidens of the goddess Frigg, often depicted attending to her. Snorri Sturluson, in the *Prose Edda*, lists Eir as one of the ásynjur (goddesses). Her association with Frigg suggests a position of importance and trust within the Norse pantheon. Beyond her role as a handmaiden, Eir is also depicted as a skilled physician, knowledgeable in the use of herbs and other remedies. Some interpretations suggest she might have been a Valkyrie, choosing who would live or die in battle, thus wielding power over life and death through her healing abilities. Although direct myths about Eir are lacking, her presence underscores the importance of health and healing in Norse society and mythology. Her existence provides insight into the value placed on medical knowledge and the potential for female figures to hold positions of authority and skill in this domain. She represents the hope for recovery and the power of compassion in the face of illness and injury. Eir's quiet but significant presence in the Norse pantheon highlights the vital role of healers and the enduring human need for comfort and care.
subtitle: Goddess of Healing and Mercy

keyMyths.title: Eir Listed Among the Asynjur
keyMyths.description: Snorri Sturluson mentions Eir as one of the ásynjur, indicating her recognized status as a goddess within the Norse pantheon. This placement solidifies her position among the important female deities.

keyMyths.title: Eir's Association with Healing
keyMyths.description: Eir is primarily associated with healing and medicine, suggesting her role as a skilled physician capable of providing aid and remedies. She embodies the concept of recovery and the power of healing.

keyMyths.title: Eir as a Handmaiden of Frigg
keyMyths.description: Eir is often described as a handmaiden of Frigg, attending to the queen of the gods. This association emphasizes her importance and trustworthiness within the Norse divine hierarchy.
